examples.title: Secure Password Hashing with bcrypt
examples.description: Demonstrates secure password hashing using bcrypt with salting and key stretching
examples.code:
import bcrypt

def hash_password(password):
    # Generate salt with work factor (12 is standard secure)
    salt = bcrypt.gensalt(rounds=12)
    # Hash password with salt (automatically includes salt in hash)
    hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode('utf-8'), salt)
    return hashed

def verify_password(password, stored_hash):
    # Verify by rehashing password and comparing
    hashed = bcrypt.hashpw(password.encode('utf-8'), stored_hash)
    return hashed == stored_hash

# Example usage
password = "SecureP@ssw0rd!"
hashed = hash_password(password)
print(f"Hashed password: {hashed}")

# Verification
is_valid = verify_password(password, hashed)
print(f"Password valid: {is_valid}")
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates secure password storage using bcrypt. It automatically generates a unique salt for each password and uses key stretching (12 rounds) to make hashing computationally expensive. The verify_password function compares the computed hash with the stored hash without ever storing the plain text password.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^r) where r is the work factor (12)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is password length

examples.title: Simple Password Hashing in C
examples.description: Demonstrates basic password hashing concept using a simple hash function
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates the basic concept of salting and hashing. It generates a random salt, combines it with the password, and produces a hash. Note: This is for educational purposes - in production, use established libraries like bcrypt or OpenSSL.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is password length

examples.title: Login Authentication Algorithm
examples.description: Complete login authentication process with security measures
examples.code:
ALGORITHM SecureLogin
INPUT: username, password
OUTPUT: Access granted/denied

BEGIN
  // Rate limiting check
  IF failed_attempts[username] >= MAX_ATTEMPTS THEN
    RETURN "Account locked - too many failed attempts"
  END IF
  
  // Retrieve user record from database
  user = get_user_from_db(username)
  IF user NOT FOUND THEN
    increment_failed_attempts(username)
    RETURN "Invalid username or password"
  END IF
  
  // Get stored hash and salt
  stored_hash = user.password_hash
  salt = user.salt
  
  // Hash input password with stored salt
  input_hash = hash_password(password, salt)
  
  // Constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks
  IF constant_time_compare(input_hash, stored_hash) THEN
    // Successful login
    reset_failed_attempts(username)
    log_successful_login(username)
    RETURN "Access granted"
  ELSE
    // Failed login
    increment_failed_attempts(username)
    log_failed_login(username)
    RETURN "Invalid username or password"
  END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows a secure login process with multiple security features: rate limiting (account lockout after failed attempts), salted password hashing, constant-time comparison (prevents timing attacks), and logging for audit trails. This represents industry-standard authentication practices.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for hashing + O(1) for lookups
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) plus O(n) for failed attempts tracking

examples.title: Password Strength Validator
examples.description: Validates password against security policy requirements
examples.code:
import re

def validate_password_strength(password):
    """Validate password against security policy"""
    errors = []
    
    # Check minimum length
    if len(password) < 8:
        errors.append("Password must be at least 8 characters")
    
    # Check for uppercase
    if not re.search(r"[A-Z]", password):
        errors.append("Password must contain uppercase letter")
    
    # Check for lowercase
    if not re.search(r"[a-z]", password):
        errors.append("Password must contain lowercase letter")
    
    # Check for digits
    if not re.search(r"\d", password):
        errors.append("Password must contain a digit")
    
    # Check for special characters
    if not re.search(r"[!@#$%^&*(),.?\":{}|<>]", password):
        errors.append("Password must contain special character")
    
    # Check common passwords list
    common_passwords = ["password", "12345678", "qwerty", "admin", "letmein"]
    if password.lower() in common_passwords:
        errors.append("Password is too common")
    
    if errors:
        return False, errors
    return True, ["Password meets all requirements"]

# Example usage
test_passwords = ["weak", "Password1", "Str0ng!Pass", "P@ssw0rd123"]
for pwd in test_passwords:
    valid, messages = validate_password_strength(pwd)
    print(f"Password: {pwd} -> Valid: {valid}, {messages}")
examples.explanation: This code implements a password strength validator that enforces common security policy requirements. It checks for minimum length, uppercase, lowercase, digits, special characters, and common passwords. This is useful for implementing password policies at registration or password change time.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is password length
